titleStmt.title: Letter from Thomas Gill to John Redmond, 12 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Thomas Patrick Gill

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Thomas Gill (1858-1931) writes to John Redmond (1856-1918) regarding his food production application, which was rejected by the Treasury. Gill appeals to Redmond to help him overturn the decision.
In 1916 Thomas Patrick Gill served as the secretary for the Department of Agriculture. He also worked with the Department of Technical Instruction from its establishment in 1900 until 1923.
John Redmond (1856-1918) was an MP and leader of the Irish Parliamentary Party. He was instrumental in securing the promise of Home Rule from Asquith's liberal government. Upon the outbreak of war, Redmond addressed a group of Irish Volunteers at Woodenbridge, Co. Wicklow, encouraging them to fight with the British Army to ensure the implementation of Home Rule. Redmond's words split the Irish Volunteers across the country. The Easter Rising shocked Redmond, who condemned it bitterly. Nonetheless he appealed for leniency towards those who had not been involved in planning the Rising.

msIdentifier.collection: John Redmond Papers, Collection List No. 118; MS 15,193/3/6

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Private
Confidential DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE
AND TECHNICAL INSTRUCTION,
DUBLIN. 12 April, 1916 My dear Redmond, I enclose you copy of Montagu's reply, which I
received this morning from Russell, to our food-production
application; and a copy of my letter dealing with it. You will see that this matter, which should be a
Cabinet matter, has been turned down by Montagu on his
own responsibility on the narrowest Treasury grounds
and quite clearly without having given any attention
to the facts of the case. This is a question of the Imperial food-supply
during the war. Runciman is the Minister who has the most
knowledge — and the best grasp — of this problem, and
you will have seen from my letter of yesterday the view
he takes. The sum asked for is the merest trifle compared
with the interests involved. I think you should see both Runciman and McKenna,
and get the thing brought forward as a Cabinet matter.
Runciman I feel sure (from my previous talks with him)
would J.E.Redmond,Esq.,M.P.
would raise it himself. The natural person to raise it,
if it were a purely Irish question, would be Birrell.
The matter is officially before the Treasury so that
McKenna could raise it — unless he decided to grant it
without doing so. You might have a word with Birrell also. I have
written to Russell, and I am counting on him to back the
thing up heartily. But you know the situation, and unless
you take it in hand I don't think much will come of it. Yours ever, T.P. Gill
